backend_selection_principle: Backend choice is made from the atomic goal templates observed in the training split or requested by a validated temporal transition. MOOSE is the first positive singleton predicate backend candidate because its method learns goal-regression rules from singleton goal conditions; other backends remain candidates only when their artifacts pass parser, binding, ASL compilation, and held-out validation gates.

benchmark_sources.coverage: IPC benchmark directories used for selected classical PDDL domains
benchmark_sources.name: potassco/pddl-instances

benchmark_sources.coverage: MOOSE benchmark data for singleton-regression-friendly domains
benchmark_sources.name: DillonZChen/moose-dataset

benchmark_sources.coverage: D2L benchmark domains used as reputable GP-family PDDL sources
benchmark_sources.name: rleap-project/d2l

claim_statement: The current paper does not claim a new universal generalized planner. It claims a framework that learns or imports domain-level lifted atomic predicate/literal plan templates from SOTA generalized-planning backends, maintains one AgentSpeak(L) library per PDDL domain, and appends query-specific temporal wrapper goals only after a lifted LTLf specification compiles to a DFA whose relevant transitions are singleton literals.

domain_count_assessment.current_paper_positioning: The benchmark infrastructure targets six selected achievement-goal planning domains. The groups describe goal properties that stress the compiler; they do not route domains to backends.
domain_count_assessment.prior_broad_gp_domain_count_range: Prior GP papers commonly evaluate around 6-13 standard or paper-defined planning families for a broad method claim; this paper deliberately uses six because the contribution is a caller/compiler framework around existing GP backends, not another universal GP learner.

evaluation_policy.main_claim_requires: A domain counts only if its atomic library is domain-level, contains no synthetic achievement names, validates on held-out problems without runtime full-trace planning, and any temporal wrapper passes singleton-literal DFA validation.
not_counted_as_main_claim: Raw external GP policies that have not passed ASL parser, binding, compiler, and validation gates.
Classical planner traces used only as offline synthesis evidence or baseline evidence.
Temporal LTLf cases whose DFA transitions contain compound guards or unsupported negative literals.

excluded_or_boundary_domains.reason: The current ASL compiler targets predicates and literals; numeric fluent semantics require a separate compiler contract.
These domains may require richer reachability, resource, or instrument reasoning before their backend artifacts can be safely compiled into ASL.
Useful future evaluation breadth, but excluded from the current six-domain focused scope.

The current compiler does not yet support graph-search or permutation-progress controllers. Putting one tile at its target square depends on blank reachability and solvability-preserving moves, not only on local producer macros or support-clearing recursion.

formal_properties.definition: A training corpus is suitable for a MOOSE-style atomic backend when the relevant reusable targets can be represented as singleton positive predicate goals, allowing first-order regression rules to explain how to achieve each literal template.
formal_properties.name: Singleton goal-regression suitability
formal_properties.paper_use: Justifies using MOOSE first for positive singleton predicate/literal template generation.

formal_properties.definition: The domain may require many objects and repeated applications of the same lifted atomic templates, but the final artifact must remain a domain-level lifted library rather than a set of problem-specific grounded traces.
formal_properties.name: Multi-object lifted template composition
formal_properties.paper_use: Separates multi-object classical achievement evaluation from singleton-only sanity checks.

formal_properties.definition: Some achievement goals are ordinary final-state PDDL literals but require support, clearing, lifting, or stacking relations to be achieved in a valid internal order. The compiler handles this by adding schema-augmented recursive atomic modules when PDDL add effects and progress certificates justify them.
formal_properties.name: Support-dependent construction need
formal_properties.paper_use: Explains why Blocks and Depots stress the compiler beyond direct singleton macro preservation.

formal_properties.definition: A backend artifact can enter the final library only when its symbols bind to declared PDDL predicates, primitive actions, predicate subgoals, or validated temporal wrapper calls without synthetic achievement names.
formal_properties.name: Safe lifted ASL compilability
formal_properties.paper_use: Defines the compiler gate between external GP artifacts and the maintained AgentSpeak(L) library.

goal_property_groups.backend_route: not_by_group; backend is selected from atomic templates
goal_property_groups.current_evidence: Both domains are present in MOOSE or IPC sources and materialized with deterministic train/test splits.
goal_property_groups.formal_membership: Problem goals whose reusable training targets are primarily positive singleton predicate templates and whose known GP evidence includes MOOSE-style goal regression.
goal_property_groups.name: Singleton-regression-friendly classical goals
goal_property_groups.paper_role: Tests the atomic template learner in domains where singleton goal regression should be a strong fit.

goal_property_groups.backend_route: not_by_group; backend is selected from atomic templates
goal_property_groups.current_evidence: Both domains are standard IPC or GP-paper domains with local train/test snapshots.
goal_property_groups.formal_membership: Problem goals that require repeated lifted atomic templates across multiple objects while still being represented as ordinary classical PDDL achievement goals.
goal_property_groups.name: Multi-object classical achievement goals
goal_property_groups.paper_role: Tests whether the maintained ASL library remains lifted and domain-level as object counts grow.

goal_property_groups.backend_route: not_by_group; backend is selected from atomic templates
goal_property_groups.current_evidence: Blocks and Depots are selected because their PDDL schemas expose support-dependent construction goals whose internal modules can be justified from action add effects and progress certificates.
goal_property_groups.formal_membership: Problem goals whose literals depend on support, clearing, lifting, stacking, or containment relations, so a compact ASL library needs schema-augmented internal producible fluent modules.
goal_property_groups.name: Support-dependent construction goals
goal_property_groups.paper_role: Tests whether the validated policy-lifting compiler can go beyond preserving MOOSE macros and emit compact support-dependent atomic modules.

goal_specification_layers.definition: The problem objective provides final-state PDDL literals. The framework extracts singleton predicate/literal templates such as on(X,Y), clear(X), at(P,L), or served(P) and builds reusable lifted AgentSpeak(L) modules for those atomic goals.
goal_specification_layers.name: Positive achievement-goal atomic template layer
goal_specification_layers.paper_use: This layer trains and validates the domain-level atomic library.
goal_specification_layers.pddl_relation: Ordinary classical PDDL problem goals define this layer directly when their goal literals can be decomposed into atomic predicate/literal templates.

goal_specification_layers.definition: The external Input module produces lifted LTLf JSON. The LTLf formula is compiled to a DFA, the DFA is validated so each relevant transition is one literal, and a query-specific +!g_query wrapper is appended to the same domain library.
goal_specification_layers.name: LTLf temporal-extended-goal wrapper layer
goal_specification_layers.paper_use: This is a goal-specification layer above the same domain library, not a domain class and not a GP backend route.
goal_specification_layers.pddl_relation: Classical PDDL :goal does not express arbitrary temporal extended goals. PDDL3 trajectory constraints cover part of this space, but this project uses stored LTLf compiled to DFA controllers.

Bonet2024GeneralPoliciesWidth.role: Provides the planning-width and sketch-theoretic basis for bounded serialized subgoal structure.
Bonet2024GeneralPoliciesWidth.title: General Policies, Subgoal Structure, and Planning Width

Bonet2024PolicyReuse.role: Motivates reusable policy modules and calls, the closest prior representation to predicate-goal ASL modules.
Bonet2024PolicyReuse.title: On Policy Reuse: An Expressive Language for Representing and Executing General Policies that Call Other Policies

Chen2026Moose.role: Defines TGI, SGI, OGI and uses ESHO domains such as Barman, Ferry, Gripper, Logistics, Miconic, Rovers, Satellite, and Transport, with path-finding components removed from some domains.
Chen2026Moose.title: Satisficing and Optimal Generalised Planning via Goal Regression

Drexler2022LearningSketches.role: Learns sketches with ASP over IPC-style domains such as Blocks-clear, Blocks-on, Childsnack, Delivery, Gripper, Miconic, Reward, Spanner, and Visitall.
Drexler2022LearningSketches.title: Learning Sketches for Decomposing Planning Problems into Subproblems of Bounded Width

Drexler2024SubgoalSketches.role: Uses handcrafted sketches for IPC domains with provable well-formedness and small sketch width, including Barman, Childsnack, Driverlog, Floortile, Grid variants, Schedule, and Traveling Purchaser Problem.
Drexler2024SubgoalSketches.title: Expressing and Exploiting Subgoal Structure in Classical Planning Using Sketches

Frances2021LearningGeneralPolicies.role: Learns feature-definable policies for Qclear, Qon, full Blocksworld, Gripper, Spanner, Miconic, Visitall, and Delivery; provides the strongest Blocksworld support precedent.
Frances2021LearningGeneralPolicies.title: Learning General Planning Policies from Small Examples Without Supervision

SegoviaAguas2022BFGPpp.role: Uses numeric and STRIPS domains including Blocks, Corridor, Floyd, Gripper, Spanner, and Visitall to evaluate terminating program synthesis.
SegoviaAguas2022BFGPpp.title: Representation and Synthesis of C++ Programs for Generalized Planning

Yang2022PG3.role: Uses six PDDL domains: Forest, Delivery, Miconic, Gripper, Ferry, and Spanner, and evaluates learned lifted decision-list policies directly on held-out problems.
Yang2022PG3.title: PG3: Policy-Guided Planning for Generalized Policy Generation

prior_benchmark_practice.implication_for_this_paper: Use goal-independent domains as the goal-separable and serialisable selected class and as MOOSE-style baselines. Do not treat them as covered unless they pass the same compiled-ASL protocol.
prior_benchmark_practice.property_axis: goal independence and Easy-to-Solve Hard-to-Optimise structure
prior_benchmark_practice.selection_style: The paper first defines TGI, SGI, and OGI, then evaluates domains expected to fit or stress those assumptions. Rovers and Transport remove path-finding components for the main goal-regression setting.

prior_benchmark_practice.implication_for_this_paper: Use bounded sketch width as the main property basis. A credible broad evaluation should include more than one standard sketchable family.
prior_benchmark_practice.property_axis: learned well-formed sketches with bounded sketch width
prior_benchmark_practice.selection_style: The paper chooses tractable IPC-style classes where small training instances can be fully explored and a learned sketch can be checked by SIWR on larger held-out instances.

prior_benchmark_practice.implication_for_this_paper: State support over problem classes Q_D and explicit properties, not over arbitrary domains. Use unsupported route or numeric fragments as boundary rows.
prior_benchmark_practice.property_axis: handcrafted sketches, subgoal structure, and bounded effective width
prior_benchmark_practice.selection_style: The papers analyze problem classes Q_D, prove or test sketch well-formedness and small width, and use standard benchmark suites where the sketch property is expected to matter.

prior_benchmark_practice.implication_for_this_paper: Blocksworld is the right core goal-dependency domain, but one standard family is narrower than this precedent.
prior_benchmark_practice.property_axis: feature-definable terminating general policies learned from small examples
prior_benchmark_practice.selection_style: The paper evaluates problem classes where a finite description-logic feature pool can separate good transitions and defines a terminating policy, including full Blocksworld with goal-dependent construction.

prior_benchmark_practice.implication_for_this_paper: A broad executable-policy claim should include several standard domains; Ferry, Gripper, Miconic, and Spanner are natural comparators.
prior_benchmark_practice.property_axis: goal-conditioned lifted decision-list policies executed directly on held-out problems
prior_benchmark_practice.selection_style: The paper evaluates policy execution on held-out problems with more objects than training, across domains suited to the lifted decision-list language.

prior_benchmark_practice.implication_for_this_paper: Program-synthesis papers support larger benchmark breadth; our narrower artifact-specific claim must be explicit unless we add more compiled-ASL domains.
prior_benchmark_practice.property_axis: terminating generalized programs with loops and validation on large instances
prior_benchmark_practice.selection_style: The paper groups domains by whether compact loop programs can solve all input instances and then validates the synthesized programs on larger instances.

selection_principle: The six-domain corpus is selected to cover goal-property regimes needed by the framework rather than to route whole domains to different GP algorithms. Domains are materialized from pinned reputable sources: potassco/pddl-instances, DillonZChen/moose-dataset, and bonetblai/learner-policies-from-examples. Each local domain snapshot has a domain.pddl file, all selected source instances, a deterministic floor(2/3 * N) training split, and a remaining held-out goal-specification split.
